site.name: Shovon Mandal

site.goal: My academic goal is to pursue advanced graduate study (M.Sc./Ph.D.) in secure distributed systems, payment security, contactless transaction security, and hardware-oriented computing. I aim to contribute to rigorous research, teaching support, technical implementation, and scholarly collaboration through a profile that combines peer-reviewed publications, secure payment prototypes, undergraduate teaching experience, academic automation systems, and hardware-oriented project engagement.

paragraphs: I am a Computer Science and Engineering lecturer whose profile combines secure computing research, implementation work, and university teaching.

For a research supervisor, my strongest value is the combination of research alignment, hands-on prototype development, publication evidence, and teaching/lab readiness. I can contribute to a research group not only as a student, but also as an implementation-focused collaborator who can document, test, teach, and mentor.

futureResearchPlan.lead: My future research direction grows directly from my thesis and prototype work: secure digital payments that connect blockchain-based verification with contactless and hardware-supported transaction environments.

cards.title: Research Problem I Want to Work On
cards.text: Cashless and contactless payments are becoming common, but many low-value payment settings still face problems of trust, tampering, device-level security, transaction traceability, and practical deployment cost. I want to study how blockchain-based verification can be combined with NFC/RFID or embedded hardware interaction to make small digital payments more secure, auditable, and usable.

cards.title: Planned Research Direction
cards.text: My planned direction is blockchain-assisted secure payment architecture for contactless and hardware-oriented systems. The work can include secure transaction logging, lightweight authentication, device-to-system trust, fraud resistance, smart-contract-based verification, and secure interaction between payment software and physical modules.

cards.title: How I Want to Approach the Work
cards.text: I am interested in building and evaluating working prototypes rather than limiting the work to a conceptual model. A possible study can include system design, threat modeling, prototype implementation, transaction testing, latency and cost analysis, and comparison with existing payment-security approaches.

cards.title: Fit with a Research Lab
cards.text: This plan fits research groups working on cybersecurity, distributed systems, blockchain, embedded/IoT systems, secure human-computer interaction, and applied computing. I can contribute through literature review, prototype development, experiment documentation, technical writing, teaching support, and collaborative publication work.
